title: Preterm Premature Rupture of Membranes
chief_complaint: I'm 29 weeks pregnant. I felt a gush of fluid from my vagina while I was sleeping. Now I'm having some cramping and a little bleeding.

physical_exam.Abdomen: Fundus 29cm. Mild contractions q5-7min on tocometer. Uterine tenderness absent.
physical_exam.Pelvic: Sterile speculum exam: Pooling of clear fluid in posterior vaginal fornix. Ferning pattern on microscopy. Nitrazine test positive (pH >7 - amniotic fluid). Cervix: 1cm dilated, 50% effaced (digital exam NOT performed - risk of infection).
physical_exam.Fetal: Fetal HR 155, reactive. No decelerations. BPP 8/8.

hidden_data.ob_us: Singleton, cephalic, AFI 3cm (severe oligohydramnios - normal >8cm). Fetal growth at 40th percentile. No anomalies.
hidden_data.amniocentesis: Amniotic fluid: Gram stain negative, Glucose 25 (normal >20), WBC 5 (normal). Culture pending.

correct_diagnosis: Preterm Premature Rupture of Membranes (PPROM) at 29 Weeks with Oligohydramnios - Rule Out Chorioamnionitis
key_learning_points: PPROM = rupture of membranes before 37 weeks and before onset of labor. Accounts for 30% of preterm births. Most common cause: Intra-amniotic infection (subclinical chorioamnionitis)

Diagnosis: Sterile speculum exam (pooling of fluid, ferning, Nitrazine test). AVOID digital cervical exam - increases infection risk and shortens latency. Ultrasound: Oligohydramnios (AFI <5 or deepest pocket <2cm). Amniocentesis if infection suspected (Gram stain, culture, glucose, WBC, IL-6)

Management depends on gestational age: <24 weeks: Counsel about poor prognosis (pulmonary hypoplasia if membrane rupture prolonged), expectant management or termination. 24-34 weeks: Expectant management with antibiotics, steroids, fetal monitoring. ≥34 weeks: Deliver (risks of prematurity lower than risks of infection)

Expectant management: 1) Betamethasone 12mg IM q24h ×2 doses (fetal lung maturity). 2) Antibiotics: Ampicillin 2g IV q6h + Azithromycin 1g PO single dose (or Erythromycin) ×7 days - prolongs latency, reduces chorioamnionitis, neonatal sepsis, and IVH. GBS prophylaxis if indicated. 3) Latency antibiotics NOT needed if GBS negative and no infection. 4) Inpatient monitoring: Daily NST/BPP, watch for infection (maternal fever, fetal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, purulent discharge, rising WBC/CRP)

Delivery indications: Chorioamnionitis (fever + uterine tenderness + maternal/fetal tachycardia + purulent discharge), Non-reassuring fetal status, Labor, Placental abruption, ≥34 weeks. Mode: Vaginal delivery preferred. C-section for standard obstetric indications. Chorioamnionitis = clinical diagnosis - DELIVER regardless of gestational age
